chunk_summary: This section outlines follow-up cadence and monitoring metrics for feeding support in neurodisability, stratified by risk tiers, with practical guidelines for review frequency and standardized metrics to track progress.
learning_objectives: Understand how to stratify patients with neurodisability into risk tiers for feeding support.
Learn the recommended follow-up cadence for each risk tier, including high, moderate, and low risk.
Identify standardized monitoring metrics such as EDACS, IDDSI levels, and respiratory burden.
Explore tele-follow-up strategies for patients with travel barriers.
key_facts: High-risk patients should be reviewed every 2–4 weeks until stable, then every 2–3 months.
Moderate-risk patients require follow-up every 6–8 weeks until stable, then every 3–6 months.
Low-risk patients should be reviewed every 6 months, with earlier reviews after respiratory infections.
Standardized metrics include EDACS, IDDSI levels, meal duration, hydration markers, and respiratory burden.
Infants with feeding problems need growth monitoring every 1–3 months.
Tele-follow-up can be used for stability checks, with caregivers sharing videos for assessment.
reference_quote: ‘Define a risk tier at each visit using swallow safety, efficiency, respiratory events, and caregiver feasibility, then match review frequency to risk.’ (Source: ESPGHAN Advice Guide)
document_key_topics: Risk stratification for feeding support in neurodisability
Follow-up cadence based on risk tiers
Standardized monitoring metrics for feeding and growth
Telemedicine for follow-up in resource-limited settings
